exemplar.title:
How did Aretha Franklinâs songs become anthems to 
Feminism  and Civil rights movement?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How did Aretha Franklinâs songs become anthems to 
Feminism  and Civil rights movement?

subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: The focus on Aretha Franklinâs music as anthems for feminism and Black civil-rights is clearly communicated and sustained throughout.
Headings, chapter outline, and concluding synthesis consistently return to the central purpose, aiding reader navigation.
The research question is explicitly stated and effectively connected to the overall discussion.
weaknesses: Occasional biographical digressions slightly detract from concision.
The research question remains somewhat broad, covering two major movements simultaneously.
The methodology description lacks explicit criteria for song selection and source evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the topic accurately and effectively, maintaining clear purpose and focus (band 5). The research question is clearly stated and linked to discussion but its breadth results in only partially focused analysis (band 3â4). The methodological outline is mostly complete with some evidence of informed selection but would benefit from explicit inclusion of criteria (band 3â4). On balance, the work merits a 5/6, reflecting highly effective focus and method with minor lapses.

strengths: Clear and coherent knowledge of Franklinâs repertoire and historical context beyond mere anecdote.
Generally appropriate and relevant application of primary and secondary source material to support arguments.
Terminology such as âanthem,â âempowerment,â and âracial prideâ is used accurately and consistently.
weaknesses: Several quotations are presented without rigorous interpretation, limiting depth of application.
Reliance on non-scholarly sources (e.g., Wikipedia, popular media) undermines academic rigor in places.
Some in-text citations and empirical evidence (e.g., statistics for riots) are missing or incomplete.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Source material is applied in ways that are clearly relevant and generally appropriate, and subject knowledge is coherent though occasionally narrative (band 3â4). Terminology and concepts are mostly accurate but specialist musicological terms are sparse (band 3â4). Overall, the work edges into the top of band 3â4 but shows enough clarity, coherence, and consistency to justify a 5/6.

strengths: Close readings of key songs (âRespect,â âThink,â âYoung, Gifted and Blackâ) demonstrate strong analytical insight and link lyrical features to broader social movements.
The legacy sectionâs linkage to Black Lives Matter and #MeToo illustrates excellent critical linkage across eras.
Reflection on media focus as a limitation shows thoughtful evaluation and awareness of counter-perspectives.
The metaphorical analysis connecting lyrics to civil-rights urgency reinforces a coherent, persuasive argument.
weaknesses: Some analytical passages remain descriptive and occasionally revert to biographical narrative.
Counterarguments or alternative viewpoints are only briefly addressed and could be expanded.
Scholarly depth is uneven due to reliance on popular websites for secondary sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research is consistently relevant to the research question, analysis is deep and well supported, and discussion is focused and coherent with only minor inconsistenciesâplacing it firmly at the top of band 10â12. A small number of descriptive sections and uneven source depth prevent a perfect 12, but the overall critical thinking is excellent, warranting an 11/12.

strengths: The structure is complete and clearly appropriate, with a logical flow aided by a contents table and headings.
Basic layout elementsâtitle page, word count, headings, reference listâare all present and correctly applied.
Use of section headings (e.g., reception subsection) enhances navigability.
weaknesses: Inconsistent font sizes and heading levels disrupt visual cohesion.
An abstract is missing, and some French headings appear in the table of contents.
Page numbering is not consistently visible, and occasional visible mark-up detracts from polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All compulsory elements of the extended essay structure are present and correctly applied, satisfying the descriptor for the top band. Minor inconsistencies in font, missing abstract, and page-number visibility are technical flaws but do not impair overall readability or organization, justifying a 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated on the title page and is clearly recognisable; however, it is fairly broad (âHow did Aretha Franklinâs songs become anthems â¦ at the end of the 20th century?â) and attempts to cover two large social movements simultaneously.  The ensuing discussion connects to the question, but the breadth sometimes leads to descriptive rather than sharply focused analysis.  These characteristics align best with a partially focused question in band 3-4; a mid-band award of 3 is appropriate.

Analytical passagesâparticularly the close reading of âRespect,â âThink,â and âYoung, Gifted and Blackââshow understanding of lyrical nuance, yet the commentary is frequently descriptive and occasionally repeats biography rather than interrogating textual features.  Conclusions are logical but sometimes asserted rather than evidenced.  This situates the depth of analysis at the âadequate/goodâ threshold, earning 4 within band 4-6.

Lyrics, biographies and journalistic commentary are incorporated at relevant points, and the chosen extracts usually illustrate the argument being made.  Nevertheless, several quotations are left to stand without rigorous interpretation, and some secondary sources (e.g., Genius, Wikipedia) limit academic rigour.  Application is therefore âmostly relevant and appropriateâ rather than consistently so, placing the work in band 3-4; a score of 3 reflects the mixed quality.

Basic layout elementsâtitle, word count, headings and reference listâare present.  However, inconsistent font sizing, visible mark-up such as â{3}ââ,â and the absence of visible page numbers within the submitted extract indicate technical flaws.  These are âsome missing or incorrect elements,â positioning the work in band 1-2 with a score of 2.

The essay demonstrates clear knowledge of Franklinâs repertoire and of the historical setting, moving beyond anecdote by linking songs to feminist and civil-rights milestones.  Use of context is coherent, though depth sometimes gives way to narrative.  Because sources are generally integrated to build a clear picture, the work fulfils the âclear and coherentâ standard but falls short of âhighly effectiveâ; a top-band 4 within 3-4 is justified.

The essay follows the expected EE structure (title page, abstract omitted, but chapters, conclusion, references provided).  Headings and a contents table aid navigation, though some French headings and formatting inconsistencies appear.  All compulsory sections are present, satisfying the descriptor for a âclearly appropriate and completeâ structure; a mid-band 3 is awarded to acknowledge minor formatting lapses.

The student assembles a reasonable body of primary and secondary material; the sources are relevant to the question, though several are popular websites and therefore limit scholarly depth.  The connection between research and question is clear but not always critically interrogated.  These features correspond to âadequate/goodâ research in band 4-6; the lower end (4) reflects uneven academic quality.

The student identifies a precise focusâAretha Franklinâs music as anthems for feminism and Black civil-rightsâand sustains that focus throughout the essay.  Headings, introductory rationale and concluding synthesis continually return to this central purpose, showing audience awareness and thematic relevance.  Occasional digressions (biographical detail, personal opinion) do not obscure the essential clarity of the chosen topic, so the descriptor for accurate and effective communication is mostly met, though minor lapses in concision justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

The line of argument is generally coherentâeach chapter culminates in statements about anthem status and legacyâyet evaluation of alternative viewpoints or limitations is brief and mainly confined to one short subsection.  Logical progression exists but is not always critically sophisticated.  Hence the work aligns with âdiscussion is good with effective reasoned argumentâ at the lower end of band 4-6, meriting 4.

The student outlines a methodâclose lyrical analysis supported by historical backgroundâand lists a range of primary (lyrics) and secondary sources.  Citations are provided, yet the rationale for source selection and the criteria for lyric extraction remain implicit, and many references are popular-media sites with uneven academic weight.  Overall the methodology is âmostly completeâ with some informed selection, meriting band 3-4 and a score of 3.

Key terms such as âanthem,â âempowerment,â âpatriarchal,â and âracial prideâ are used appropriately, and basic concepts from feminism and civil-rights discourse are evident.  However, specialist musicological or literary terminology is sparse, and some inaccuracies in spelling/grammar occasionally blur meaning.  Overall accuracy is âmostlyâ rather than fully consistent, warranting a score of 3 in band 3-4.

annotations.comment: The methodology description explains lyric analysis but omits criteria for song selection and source evaluation. Clarify these to demonstrate informed methodological choices.
The chapter outline is clear and helps the reader follow the structure of the analysis across feminist, racial, and legacy themes.
Heading levels and fonts vary between chapters; unify styles for consistency in presentation and navigation.
Citation âFranklin (1967)â lacks a page number or more precise reference. Provide full citation details for accuracy.
The analysis of âRespectâ insightfully links lyrics to female agency, but it could deeper interrogate the broader cultural resonance beyond the domestic sphere.

The research question is clearly stated but remains overly broad, covering two major movements simultaneously. Narrowing its scope will allow for deeper, more focused analysis relevant to specific aspects of Franklinâs impact.
The title concisely communicates the investigationâs focus on Franklinâs role in feminism and Black equality, providing clear direction for the reader.
Including a precise word count demonstrates adherence to the 4000-word limit, fulfilling the formatting requirement.
Page numbers are not visible on this page, which impedes navigation and reference. Ensure consistent pagination throughout the document.
The French heading in the table of contents may confuse an English-speaking audience. Standardize the language of headings to maintain clarity.
The introductory historical context lacks in-text citations for key statistics (e.g., number of riots, deaths). Add precise references to strengthen academic rigour.
An abstract is expected in an EE before the introduction. Including one will improve the structure and fulfill expected conventions.
Citation formatting for Wayne State University Press (1969) is inconsistent. Standardize author-date style (including page numbers if applicable).
Biographical details about Franklinâs upbringing are presented descriptively. Integrate critical evaluation of how these influences shape her lyrical themes.
Link the broader historical context more explicitly to the research question to maintain focus on Franklinâs music as social anthems.
This paragraph remains largely narrative. Develop sharper analysis by directly linking Franklinâs familial influences to feminist or civil-rights lyric content.
Contextualizing âThinkâ within Franklinâs personal experience adds depth, demonstrating clear knowledge of background influences.
Quotation from Franklinâs autobiography provides valuable source material, but the analysis could deepen by comparing it with contemporary feminist critiques.
Linking verbatim lyrics to the broader feminist movement enhances relevance. This contextual connection is a clear application of source material.
The cultural impact subsection clearly highlights modern parallels, reinforcing the essayâs relevance to contemporary movements.
Use consistent heading levels for âCultural Impactâ to maintain structural coherence with other subsections.
The Rolling Stone citation is well-selected but omits retrieval date and page details. Complete the reference for academic consistency.
Relying on Page Six for critical context limits scholarly depth. Consider replacing or supplementing with peer-reviewed sources.
The close reading of vocal tone in the refrain effectively supports your argument about empowerment, showing good analytical depth.
Font sizes for subheadings vary (e.g., âLove and Relationshipâ). Regularize to maintain a professional layout.
Analysis of âA Natural Womanâ articulates emotional impact but stops short of linking it to feminist theory. Expanding theoretical connections would strengthen the argument.
Claim that the song became an anthem for white and Black women lacks citation. Add empirical evidence or testimonies to support this assertion.
Introducing the reception subsection with a clear heading improves navigability and signals analytical shift.
The section on negative reception insightfully highlights resistance but would benefit from direct citation of criticsâ reviews.
Reframing âRespectâ as a civil-rights anthem is well-justified and demonstrates the researchâs relevance to the question.
The metaphor linking lyrics to civil-rights urgency exhibits clear analytical insight, reinforcing the anthem argument.
Contextual note on âYoung, Gifted and Blackâ linking Simone and Franklin showcases effective use of intertextual sources.
The dense block of text on page 10 hampers readability. Consider breaking into smaller paragraphs to guide readers through successive arguments.
Emotive description of Franklinâs performance effectively conveys audience impact, showing strong analytical depth.
Discussion of pivotal lyrics is cohesive, but introducing a counter-perspective (e.g., critics who saw it as less political) would deepen evaluation.
The connection between gospel roots and civil-rights meetings is insightful but requires a scholarly source citation for verification.
The quotation from President Obama powerfully affirms Franklinâs legacy, but the in-text reference lacks full citation details.
Link to Black Lives Matter and #MeToo movements strengthens the legacy argument, illustrating excellent critical linkage across eras.
Acknowledging media focus on personal scandals as a limitation introduces reflective evaluation of public perception.
Future research ideas are relevant but remain general. Propose specific comparative case studies to sharpen the investigative agenda.
The final reflection conveys personal engagement but lacks analysis of how setbacks shaped the research process. Deepen with evaluative insights.
Reference entries vary in format (URL placement, italics). Standardize using a consistent citation style (e.g., APA or MLA).
Entries including DOI and publisher details (e.g., NYU Press) demonstrate good attention to academic referencing conventions.
The Wikipedia URL in the last entry is malformed and lacks retrieval date. Replace with a stable academic source or correct the link.
